---
title: How We Use Statistics & Genetics to Explore the History of Humans
author: Eric Friedlander
---

```{r setup}
library(tidyverse)
library(mosaic)
library(reshape)
```

## A Little Bit About Me {.smaller}

- I'm in the **MAPS** (Mathematics & Physical Sciences) department
- I mostly teach Statistics, Computer Science, and Data Analytics Courses
- My undergraduate degree is in Mathematics & Statistics
- After college, I was a Business Analyst at Capital One 
- My graduate degree is in Statistics & Operations Research
- Did a Post-Doc in Population Genetics
- I grew up just outside New York City

## Why should you study Math and Statistics?

- [The demand for quantitative skills is outpacing nearly every other field]{.fragment .strike}
- [Extremely high earning potential]{.fragment .strike}

. . .

:::{.incremental}
- [Because it's hard](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oYJRwgHEFS0)
- Doing hard stuff is rewarding and will prepare you for success
- Doing hard stuff will provide you with flexibility 
:::

# Population Genetics

## Let's begin with a biology lesson!

![](images/Graphics1-part-1.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Let's begin with a biology lesson!

![](images/chromosomes.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Big Picture: Questions about evolving populations

- What was the past size and structure of populations?

![](images/outofafrica.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Big Picture: Questions about evolving populations

- Where in the genome is natural selection occurring?

![](images/lct-1.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## Sequencing Data

![](images/03GENOME3-jumbo.jpg){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}

## What do we want?

:::{.incremental}
- Look at patterns in our data $\Rightarrow$ figure out what situations would give rise to those patterns
- Work backwards:
  - Make assumptions: population size history, strength of selection, etc.
  - Input: Sequencing data
  - Output: Probability of data
:::

## How do we do this?

:::{.incremental}
- We build a model
- Assumptions \& Data $\stackrel{\text{model}}{\rightarrow}$ Probability
- What's a model? 
:::

## Text "modelyote " followed by your answer to 22333. 

E.g. *modelyote someone that wears clothes in a magazine*

<div class="container">
<iframe src="https://embed.polleverywhere.com/free_text_polls/m8v8aZDjNsGL0dtEE2CLZ?controls=none&short_poll=true" width="800px" height="600px"></iframe>
</div>


## What is a model?

- A model is a simplified but tractable version of reality
- What does tractable mean?



## Calculating the area of California

![](images/cali.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center"}


## A Model for California

![](images/cali-2.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center}

## George P. Box

::::{.columns}
:::{.column}
- From Wikipedia: Blah, blah blah, "one of the great statistical minds of the 20th century", blah blah blah.
- "all models are wrong, but some are useful"
:::
:::{.column}
![](images/GeorgeEPBox.jpg){.r-stretch fig-align="center}
:::
::::

## Let's build a model!

![](images/builder_mario_and_builder_luigi.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center}

## Simplifying Reality {.smaller}

:::{.incremental}
- Population dynamics $\Rightarrow$ super complex... let's simplify
- Focus on one location in genome (a single base-pair or gene)
- Two possibilities ("alleles") at this site: <span style="color:red;">Mario</span> and <span style="color:green;">Luigi</span>
- Fixed population size $N$
- Time measured in generations
- All offspring born at the same time
- Offspring randomly choose parent from previous generation
:::


## Let's build a *model*!

- I need some volunteers!
- Raise your hand if you're willing to come on up!


## Shiny App

- [Pop-Gen App](https://efriedlander.shinyapps.io/usf2026-shiny/)

## Wright-Fisher Model

```{r, fig.align="center"}
data <- data.frame(x = 0:8, y = c(0.5, 0.4, 0.3, .3,.3,.5,.4,.5,.5))
ggplot(data = data, aes(x =x, y= y)) +
  geom_step() +
  xlim(0, 8) +
  ylim(0, 1) +
  xlab("Generation") +
  ylab("Luigi Proportion")
```

## Statisticians know this distribution!

:::{.incremental}
-   If $p_k$ is the proportion of Luigi's in the $k$th generation and...
-   $X_{k+1}$ is the number of Luigi's in the $k+1$th generation then...
-   $X_k$ has a *Binomial distribution*
:::

## We can simulate this!

One generation:

```{r}
#| echo: TRUE

N <- 10 # Population size is 10
x1 <- 5 # Start with 5 Luigies
p1 <- x1 / N
x2 <- rbinom(1, N, p1)
x2
```

## We can simulate this!

Two generations:

```{r}
#| echo: TRUE

p2 <- x2 / N
x3 <- rbinom(1, N, p2)
x3
```

## We can simulate this!

Ten generations:

```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
x <- rep(0, 10)
x[1] <- x1
for(i in 1:9){
  p <- x[i] / N
  x[i+1] <- rbinom(1, N, p)
}
x
```

## We can simulate this!

-   What if we have 1,000 individuals in a population and we wanted to simulate it for 1,000 generations?!?

. . .

-   Pffft... easy

```{r}
#| echo: TRUE

N <- 1000
x <- rep(0, 1000)
x[1] <- 500
for(i in 1:999){
  p <- x[i] / N
  x[i+1] <- rbinom(1, N, p)
}
x
```

## A few Simulations

```{r}
set.seed(1988)
reps <- 1000
pop_size <- 1000
init <- .5
gens <- 1000
p <- matrix(nrow=reps, ncol=gens)
p[ ,1] = init
steps <- pop_size
for(i in 2:gens){
      p[, i] = rbinom(reps, size=pop_size, prob=p[,i-1])/pop_size
}

p_melted <- melt(p[1:3,])
ggplot(aes(x = X2, y = value, color=as.factor(X1)), data=p_melted) +
geom_line() +
xlab("Generations") +
ylab("Luigi Proportion")+
ylim(0, 1) +
theme(legend.position="none")
```

## Lots of Simulations

```{r}
p_melted <- melt(p)
p_melted |>
  filter(X1 < 101) |> 
ggplot(aes(x = X2, y = value, color=as.factor(X1))) +
geom_line(alpha=0.7, lwd=.3) +
xlab("Generations") +
ylab("Luigi Proportion")+
ylim(0, 1) +
theme(legend.position="none")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
ggplot(aes(x = X2, y = value, color=as.factor(X1)), data=p_melted) +
geom_line(alpha=0.7, lwd=.3) +
geom_vline(aes(xintercept=100), color="red") +
xlab("Generations") +
ylab("Luigi Proportion")+
ylim(0, 1) +
theme(legend.position="none")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
y_max <- 8
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 100) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 100 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
ggplot(aes(x = X2, y = value, color=as.factor(X1)), data=p_melted) +
geom_line(alpha=0.7, lwd=.3) +
geom_vline(xintercept=seq(100, 1000, 100), color="red") +
xlab("Generations") +
ylab("Luigi Proportion")+
ylim(0, 1) +
theme(legend.position="none")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 100) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 100 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 200) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 200 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 300) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 300 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 400) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 400 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 500) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 500 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 600) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 600 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 700) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 700 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 800) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 800 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 900) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 900 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: Cross-Section

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 1000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 1000 Generations")
```

## Adding Complexity

:::{.incremental}
- What's going to happen eventually?
  - All <span style="color:red;">Marios</span> or all <span style="color:green;">Luigis</span>
- Mutation: Occasionally <span style="color:red;">Mario</span> mutates into <span style="color:green;">Luigi</span> (and vice versa)
  - Let's add mutation to our model
:::

## How can we add mutation to our model?

- Any ideas?

## Back to the Shiny App

- [Pop-Gen App](https://efriedlander.shinyapps.io/usf2026-shiny/)

## How can we make this useful?

- Let's consider a simple question: "What is the mutation rate in humans?"

## Lots of Simulations: 100 Generations

```{r}
set.seed(1988)
reps <- 10000
pop_size <- 1000
init <- .5
gens <- 10000
mut <- 10^(-4)
final_prob <- 0
p <- matrix(nrow=reps, ncol=gens)
p[ ,1] = init
steps <- pop_size
for(i in 2:gens){
  psi <- p[,i-1]*(1-mut)+(1-p[,i-1])*mut
  p[, i] <- rbinom(reps, size=pop_size, prob=psi)/pop_size
}

p_melted <- melt(p)
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 100) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 100 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 200 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 200) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 200 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 300 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 300) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 300 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 500 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 500) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 500 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 1000 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 1000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 1000 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 2000 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 2000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 2000 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 3000 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 3000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 3000 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 4000 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 4000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 4000 Generations")
```

## Lots of Simulations: 5000 Generations

```{r}
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 5000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 5000 Generations")
```

## Stationary Distribution

```{r}
p_melted <- melt(p)
p_melted |>
filter(X2 == 5000) %>%
ggplot(aes(x = value), data = .) +
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density))) +
  # geom_density() +
  ylim(0,y_max) +
  ylab("") +
  xlab("Luigi Proportion 1000 Generations")
```

## How do we use this to answer our question?

```{r}
set.seed(1988)
mut <- 10^(-3)
p <- matrix(nrow=reps, ncol=gens)
p[ ,1] = init
steps <- pop_size
for(i in 2:gens){
  psi <- p[,i-1]*(1-mut)+(1-p[,i-1])*mut
  p[, i] <- rbinom(reps, size=pop_size, prob=psi)/pop_size
}

p_melted_large <- melt(p) |> 
  filter(X2 == 10000) |> 
  mutate(Rate = "Big")

mut <- 10^(-5)
p <- matrix(nrow=reps, ncol=gens)
p[ ,1] = init
steps <- pop_size
for(i in 2:gens){
  psi <- p[,i-1]*(1-mut)+(1-p[,i-1])*mut
  p[, i] <- rbinom(reps, size=pop_size, prob=psi)/pop_size
}
p_melted_small <- melt(p) |> 
  filter(X2 == 10000) |> 
  mutate(Rate = "Small")

p_melted |>
  mutate(Rate = "Medium") |> 
  bind_rows(p_melted_large) |>
  bind_rows(p_melted_small) |>
  filter(X2 == 10000) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density), color=Rate), fill="white", alpha=0, position="identity") +
    # geom_density(aes(color=Rate), alpha=0) +
    # ylim(0,y_max) +
    ylab("") +
    xlab("Luigi Proportion Steady State")
```

## Back to the app!

[Pop-Gen App](https://efriedlander.shinyapps.io/usf2026-shiny/)

# MORE COMPLEXITY!

## Three More Evoluationary "Forces"

- Recombination
- Natural Selection
  - Recombination + Natural Selection = Genetic Hitchhiking
- Migration

## Genetic Hitchhiking

![](images/WF-Images/Hitch1.jpg){.r-stretch fig-align="center}


## Cross-Over Recombination

![](images/crossover-recombination.png){.r-stretch fig-align="center}


## Genetic Hitchhiking

![](images/WF-Images/Hitch2.jpg){.r-stretch fig-align="center}


## Genetic Hitchhiking

![](images/WF-Images/Hitch3.jpg){.r-stretch fig-align="center}

## Discussion

:::{.incremental}
- Why can't we capture Genetic Hitchhiking with our model? (Two main reasons)
  + Only one location
  + No selection
- Any ideas for how we could incorporate selection in our model?
- What about recombination?
:::

## Recap

- Models are simplified but tractable version of reality
- Modeling can be fun (especially when you practice it)
- I've hidden a lot of the underlying math from you and there are lot of additional considerations to build models

## Wrap-Up {.smaller}

- Math and Statistics is applicable to almost everything
- In the past year I've worked on projects (most with students) in the following fields:
  + Genetics (Both Population and Statistical)
  + Physics
  + Metabolomics (Chemistry)
  + Ecology
  + Education/Psychology
  + Paleo-anthropology
  + Business
  + Computer Science


## Programs In Our Department


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column}
## Majors

- Mathematics
- Physics - Mathematics
- Computer Science - Mathematics
- Data Analytics (new 3+1 program)



:::

::: {.column}

## Minors:

- Data Science
:::

::::
